content: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is damage to the spinal cord causing temporary or permanent neurological deficits. Incidence ~17,000/year in US, predominantly young males. Mechanisms: traumatic (motor vehicle accidents, falls, violence, sports) vs non-traumatic (tumor, infection, vascular, degenerative). Classification by level: tetraplegia/quadriplegia (cervical, affects arms and legs), paraplegia (thoracic/lumbar/sacral, affects legs only). Completeness: complete (no motor/sensory function below injury including sacral segments S4-S5) vs incomplete (some preservation). Incomplete syndromes: central cord (upper > lower extremity weakness, often hyperextension in elderly with stenosis), Brown-Séquard (hemisection—ipsilateral motor/proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temp loss), anterior cord (motor and pain/temp loss, preserved proprioception), cauda equina (LMN signs, bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia). Spinal shock: transient flaccidity and areflexia immediately post-injury, resolves over days-weeks with return of reflexes and possible spasticity.
